data.title: 13 new Hong Kong restaurants to try in December 2025, from Japanese to steak
data.description:
If you are looking for an excuse to venture to different parts of the city, a new restaurant is as good a reason as any.
Among the latest openings include a Japanese kappou restaurant at The Henderson building in Central, as well as outlets offering pre- or post-event meals at the East Kowloon Cultural Centre and Kai Tak Sports Park.
Two new authentic Cantonese eateries in the style of dai pai dongs have also opened in Wan Chai.
Read on to see where to satisfy your culinary curiosity in December.
1. Hana no Kumo
Perched on the 38th floor of The Henderson in Central, Hana no Kumo offers an extraordinary Japanese kappou (a multi-course meal cooked in front of the diner) experience.
Under the guidance of executive chef Ogawa Masaru, the menu celebrates seasonal ingredients and features exquisite dishes such as steamed abalone and sea urchin with seaweed sauce, sashimi, and donabe (claypot) rice with black truffles.
Summit 38, 38/F, The Henderson, 2 Murray Road, Central
2. Torikaze

Landmark Hong Kong welcomes the arrival of yakitori restaurant Torikaze to Central.
This debut marks the first Hong Kong venture from revered master chef Yoshiteru Ikegawa of the notoriously hard-to-book Torishiki Ichimon in Tokyo.
The restaurant focuses on technical perfection, grilling humble ingredients over pure binchotan charcoal.
The menu elevates simple skewers into a sensorial encounter, where each bite, from classic chicken cuts to charred seasonal vegetables, is executed to incredible precision.
43-45/F, Forty-Five, Gloucester Tower, Landmark, 15 Queen’s Road Central
3. Petit Jardin

Petit Jardin in The Peak Tower offers a contemporary and casual take on Shanghainese and Jiangnan cuisine.
As a subsidiary of the well-known Jardin De Jade, the restaurant features creative dishes including the signature Jiangnan gua bao (literally “cut bun”) platter and the must-try Shanghai juicy trio assorted xiaolongbao.
With innovative cocktails and sweeping views of Victoria Harbour, Petit Jardin provides an elegant yet relaxed setting for enjoying a modern taste of Shanghai high above Hong Kong.
Shop 103, Level 1, The Peak Tower, 128 Peak Road, The Peak
4. Gyorai
Kumamoto’s popular tsukemen (dipping ramen) shop will open its first overseas offshoot in Wan Chai on December 3, and long queues are expected.
Must-tries include the eatery’s signature spicy miso tsukemen in a blended pork and seafood broth.
Another popular favourite is the curry tsukemen, which is cooked in the same special broth blend.
389 Lockhart Road, Wan Chai
5. Lai’s Kitchen
If you are missing the wok hei (“breath of the wok”) of stir-fries or the crunchiness of claypot rice, then head to Lai’s Kitchen on Thompson Road in Wan Chai, opened by the same team behind Flower Drum and Fat J’s Char Siu.
Dig into dishes such as typhoon shelter mantis shrimp, deep-fried squid, and hawthorn and olive spare ribs.
Crisp claypot rice is a draw here, with over a dozen to choose from such as perilla leaf, black bean and eel, or minced beef with egg.
There is something for everyone at this late-night joint.
1/F, Harvard Commercial Building, 105-111 Thomson Road, Wan Chai
6. Boat King
If you do not feel like cooking at home but are craving homestyle dishes, Boat King on Ship Street is a new option, providing fresh seafood and solid stir-fries for the whole family.
Sink your teeth into diced black pepper beef with potatoes as well as pepper pig stomach soup and soft and silky congees.
Shop 1, Pao Yip Building, 1-7 Ship Street, Wan Chai
7. Kyanbasu by Mt. Yotei
Lamb enthusiasts rejoice! Those familiar with the original Mt. Yotei lamb yakiniku (grilled meat) restaurant in Causeway Bay will be happy to find that they have opened an omakase offshoot in Central.
Feast on special menu items all made with lamb, such as skewers, char siu, dumplings, soupy curry and more.
40 Gough Street, Central
8. La Baia

La Baia is a vibrant new all-day destination bringing the comfort of Italian cuisine to the waterfront Dining Cove at Kai Tak Sports Park.
This new spot, with its stylish coastal-inspired interior and sunny alfresco patio, offers a menu showcasing house-made pastas and 24-hour fermented Neapolitan pizzas.
Guests can look forward to rich, shareable mains like herb roast chicken, or savour the decadent sea urchin aglio olio linguine, coated in a creamy umami emulsion.
DC-010, Dining Cove, Kai Tak Sports Park , Kai Tak, Kowloon.
9. Mi Manchi

Tucked away in the East Kowloon Cultural Centre, Mi Manchi is a heartfelt new Italian restaurant that offers classic dishes with a Chinese twist.
The menu weaves local ingredients into Italian classics. Alongside handmade pastas and 24-hour sourdough pizzas, dishes like the spaghetti alla red shrimp – featuring seafood marinated in huadiao wine and served tableside with an aromatic wine spray – showcase a delightful and surprising culinary perspective.
1/F, East Kowloon Cultural Centre, 60 Ngau Tau Kok Road, Kowloon
10. Zozzona

A Japanese pasta specialist has opened up in Tsim Sha Tsui.
Fresh pasta is made on the premises daily. Diners can enjoy traditional options such as linguine vongole and tagliolini freddi, or try something with a Japanese twist like Kurobuta pork meatballs.
Flat A, Quality Tower, 29-31 Hillwood Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui
11. La Vache!

Steak frites specialist La Vache! has opened its largest Hong Kong branch yet at Exchange Square.
The concept remains the same and uncomplicated: a single menu featuring a crisp organic salad followed by perfectly cooked USDA prime rib-eye steak and a bottomless supply of golden frites with signature sauce.
This new, spacious outpost also introduces a vibrant bar serving hearty snacks like the decadent steak sandwich.
Shop 103, 1 Two Exchange Square, Connaught Rd Central
12. Sushi Raku

A prominent restaurant chain in Hong Kong’s sushi scene, Sushi Raku has opened a new outlet in the dynamic BaseHall 01 food hall.
Renowned as the city’s bluefin tuna specialist, the restaurant sources directly from Tokyo’s Toyosu Market and secures the finest fish daily from legendary tuna wholesaler Yamayuki.
Diners can savour wet-aged tuna and other seasonal selections.
BaseHall 01, Shop LG9, LG/F, Jardine House, 1 Connaught Place, Central
13. Emmer Pizza Central
The acclaimed Emmer Pizza, celebrated for its artisanal crusts, is expanding its footprint with a new branch at Landmark in Central.
After winning a loyal following in Admiralty, this new location brings its signature pizzas to the heart of the city’s bustling business and shopping district.
Diners can enjoy tried-and-true favourites such as the funghi, parma and carnivore pizza, or try new creations such as Thai-flavoured pad kra pao.
Shop 217A, 2/F, Landmark Atrium, 15 Queen’s Road Central

data.title: Ex-UC Berkeley scientist dies aged 44 amid growing concern over toll on Chinese academics
data.description:
A Chinese biomedical researcher whose academic trajectory included years at the University of California, Berkeley, and collaborations with pioneering chemical engineers has died at the age of 44.
Xie Hongxue, who had been working as a lecturer for nearly five years at the school of materials science and engineering at West Anhui University, a small regional college in central China, died in Wuhan on November 16 from an unnamed illness, according to Shanghai-based news site ThePaper.cn.
Xie’s journey reflects a growing paradox in Chinese higher education. Despite elite overseas training and a prolific research record, some returnees now face diminishing prospects, ending up in under-resourced universities with heavy workloads and limited support.
He is among the dozens of younger Chinese academics who have died this year, an issue that has raised further questions about the country’s cutthroat academic system.
He spent several years at Berkeley, studying under John Prausnitz, who is considered the father of molecular thermodynamics in chemical engineering.
His work helped make modern chemical manufacturing processes more energy-efficient, safer and less polluting, according to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
Xie’s research focus was on biochemistry and the green chemical industry. At the time of his death, he was working on four research projects, according to ThePaper.
He published more than 10 articles in leading journals, was granted seven invention patents and 30 utility model patents and co-authored two books, according to his university profile.
His published works covered the use of plant species to improve chemical processes and the development of nanoparticles and nanocomposites. The patents included a device to separate and purify animal serum proteins and an adeno-associated virus purification kit.
In May, Xie helped lead a team from West Anhui University to the Chinese vocational education innovation and entrepreneurship competition, where they won first place in the undergraduate group for their work on automated DNA sequencing, according to the university.
Xie obtained his undergraduate and master’s degrees at Henan University of Technology from 2000 to 2007, after which he completed his doctoral studies at the China University of Geosciences in Wuhan in 2011.
Between 2009 and 2011, he studied at Berkeley through a joint doctoral training programme before staying on as a postdoctoral researcher until 2014.
Xie also worked as a research scientist at US biotechnology company Alstem and helped found the Chinese branch of Quintara Biosciences.


In 2019, Xie joined Xinjiang Normal University’s school of chemical engineering as a part-time lecturer.
In June 2020, he joined West Anhui University, one of the country’s lesser-known universities, as a full-time lecturer.
While working there, he also stayed involved in the commercial sector, with roles at Anhui Dezhengtang Pharmaceutical Technology Group and as the chief scientist of the packaging company Ningbo Joy Intelligent Logistics Technology.
Xie was the recipient of a scholarship from the China Scholarship Council for overseas study, received a doctoral talent allowance from the city of Wuhan, and was selected for a municipal talent programme. He also helped to lead a provincial gene detection platform project in Hubei, according to his university profile.
Xie also served as the founding president of the Wuhan chapter of the Berkeley Chinese Alumni International Association.
In a memorial posted on social media last week, the association said he was dedicated to growing the alumni network, which included social events, exchanges and support for younger alumni.
“His dedication to alumni affairs, his commitment to serving alumni, and his steadfast commitment to unity and mutual assistance will continue to inspire every one of us to move forward firmly along the path he left unfinished,” the memorial said.
Passionate about science? Dive deeper with the Dark Matters newsletter, a weekly in-depth analysis on China’s rise in science, technology and military that goes beneath the surface. Sign up for free now.

data.title: As Philippines targets more Pogo figures, can Marcos regain public trust?
data.description:
A travel ban imposed by the Philippines against two figures tied to its notorious offshore gaming industry (Pogos) marks a bid by the Ferdinand Marcos Jnr administration to boost public standing, but the country’s unfolding flood-control scandal remains the biggest mover of political fortunes.
On Tuesday, the Department of Foreign Affairs confirmed that it had cancelled the passports of Harry Roque, a former spokesman for ex-president Rodrigo Duterte, and Cassandra Li Ong, an associate of former mayor Alice Guo.
The move came just days after Guo, a central figure linked to the Pogos sector, was sentenced to life imprisonment for human trafficking.
While Filipinos are likely to support further moves to target individuals linked to Pogos, they are more concerned about the huge sums purportedly siphoned from flood-control projects, analysts say.
Roque and Ong face trafficking charges in connection with Lucky South 99, a Pogos-linked compound, where authorities rescued nearly 200 workers in a raid last year and discovered evidence of torture, human trafficking and scam operations.
The raid took place amid a year-long government crackdown on the now-prohibited Pogos, which flourished during the previous Duterte administration.
The cancellation of passports belonging to Roque and Ong happened seven months after charges were filed against them and several days after a Philippine court sentenced Guo and seven others to life imprisonment for human trafficking, and ordered the Baofu compound in Bamban, Tarlac, to be forfeited to the government.


Ong is the authorised representative of Lucky South 99, while Roque acted as the company’s lawyer during its application for a government licence.
In charges filed against Roque last year, authorities alleged that he had taken part in “illegal activities” involving the company. Roque is reportedly seeking asylum at The Hague in the Netherlands, where he claims that he is being targeted for political persecution by Manila due to his ties with Duterte.
Ong has not been seen after her release in December from detention for contempt during a Senate hearing. The Department of Justice has offered a reward of 1 million pesos (US$17,000) for credible information on Ong’s whereabouts. The Philippine Anti-Organised Crime Commission traced her last known location to Japan in January.
“Cassie Li Ong and Harry Roque are facing serious charges of human trafficking. It is only right that their passports were cancelled, especially since they appear to be evading the law,” opposition senator Risa Hontiveros, who led investigations by a committee into alleged human trafficking by Pogos last year, said in a statement earlier this week.
“Our government must also not stop until other criminal syndicates that enabled these Pogo scam hubs are held accountable. The conviction of Alice Guo is not the end of the story. She is not the only one who must answer for these crimes – everyone involved should be held responsible.”

Flood control scandal dominates
Arjan Aguirre, an assistant professor at the Ateneo de Manila University’s political science department, said the court ruling in Guo’s case and the passport cancellation announcement were a boost to the Marcos administration in showing that it was producing “concrete results” in the crackdown against Pogos.
“These actions appear interconnected and consistent with the administration’s broader effort to project firmness and restore public confidence,” Aguirre told This Week in Asia.
Coupled with the nationwide investigations against individuals allegedly involved in the flood-control scandal, Marcos is determined to bolster public trust in his administration through his crackdown against crime and corruption, according to Aguirre.
A Pulse Asia survey in September showed that the president’s approval rating fell to 33 per cent in September from 42 per cent in June. In comparison, the rating for Vice-President Sara Duterte-Carpio fell to 55 per cent from 59 per cent over the same period.
Aguirre said the Marcos administration’s anti-corruption moves were also aimed at preventing a deterioration in the political climate to an extent that Duterte could stage a comeback.
Marcos’ approval ratings are unlikely to improve in any significant way
Arjan Aguirre, Ateneo de Manila University’s academic
Matthew David Ordonez, a political science lecturer at De La Salle University, said the actions taken against individuals linked to Pogos were long overdue following a promise made by Marcos in his State of the Nation Address last year.
“Perhaps this more aggressive turn on the Pogos can offset the flood control scandal and preserve what remaining approval the administration has among the people while reminding the people of Duterte’s role in bringing Pogos in,” Ordonez said.
However, he noted that the crackdown against Pogos had adversely affected sectors such as property and retail.
Aguirre said that the trillions of pesos lost to corruption arising from the flood-control scandal would continue to overshadow any further moves against individuals linked to Pogos.
“Even if this leads to Roque’s arrest, it won’t divert the public from the core issue – the flood-control scandal. Marcos’ approval ratings are unlikely to improve in any significant way.”

data.title: Oxfam Trailwaker cancelled, questions over Cyclothon as Hong Kong reels from fire deaths
data.description:
The 2025 Oxfam Trailwalker race that was expected to start on Friday, has reportedly been cancelled.
Organisers held a meeting this morning, and decided not to go ahead in light of the events in Tai Po, sources say.
Official confirmation is expected soon, with almost 4,000 runners, some who have travelled from overseas, waiting for news.
Separately, organisers of Sunday’s Cyclothon were also meeting on Thursday to decide if that event should also be delayed.
More to follow...

data.title: To prevent an AI job apocalypse, take a leaf from immigration policy
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
As governments rush to draft AI legislation, it is crucial to define clear principles on how far artificial intelligence should be allowed to reshape the employment landscape. Technological progress is inevitable, but policymakers must recognise that some changes will be irreversible. A laissez-faire approach risks creating long-term social and economic damage, leaving entire segments of the workforce behind.
Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton has warned that AI could wipe out humanity. While that may sound extreme, ignoring the disruptive impact on jobs is equally dangerous.
Regulation should not be reactive. Waiting until tech giants accumulate unprecedented wealth while the labour class is decimated is a recipe for inequality and instability. Instead, governments must adopt proactive frameworks that balance innovation with social responsibility, ensuring AI serves society rather than undermines it.
One possible model can be drawn from immigration policy. For decades, governments have managed foreign talent inflows through the test of labour markets, requiring proof that certain skills are unavailable locally and applying quota systems that limit foreign hires once a threshold is reached. A similar matrix-based approach could apply to AI adoption. Businesses could be required to assess the availability of local human talent before automating roles, set thresholds for automation to prevent large-scale redundancies and conduct impact assessments to evaluate workforce displacement prior to deploying AI or robotics.
These measures would ensure AI integration is responsible and socially sustainable. By introducing checks and balances, governments can prevent mass unemployment while still encouraging innovation and competitiveness. Such policies would also help maintain economic stability and protect vulnerable sectors from abrupt disruption.
The bottom line is clear: any new policy or legislation should prioritise the well-being and economic security of the majority, rather than granting unchecked advantages to the already highly profitable tech giants. AI can be a powerful tool for progress, but without thoughtful governance, it risks becoming a driver of inequality. Governments must act now to create frameworks that safeguard jobs, support reskilling and ensure technology serves humanity – and not the other way around.
Stephanie Y.K. Hasancebi, Sham Tseng
Let nature do its thing in our open spaces
The excellent letter on the rewilding of our seas (“Stingray in harbour a welcome sign of nature’s restoration”, November 20) prompts me to write a plea for rewilding on land too. Hong Kong cries out for the rewilding of open spaces.
Species loss in recent decades has been deplorable. While the Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department does a good job of maintaining our country parks, arguably it does too much. We should rewild – let nature do its thing.
As Henry David Thoreau put it: “all good things are wild and free”.
Rewilding means humans should get out of the way. Where possible, human-created infrastructure should be removed. Some beautiful plants such as bougainvillea and bauhinia may be strategically sited, but allow native flora to regenerate. This re-establishes complex habitats, in time boosting species richness.
The appearance of our landscapes will change as a mosaic of different habitats – forest, scrubland, grass, wetland – emerges. The result is a wilder, healthier, richer environment. If we do this, birds and butterflies will return in their thousands, their beauty giving us all the thanks we could want.
Roger Medcalf, Sai Kung
Obstacles to China’s economic rebalancing should be addressed
Professor Lu Feng’s analysis of China’s persistently weak domestic consumption raises several valid concerns, particularly around structural imbalances and the limits of export-driven growth (“Chinese economist Lu Feng on why the time is ripe for a consumption rebalance”, November 17). However, his arguments also contain notable gaps.
He treated domestic consumption as primarily a demand-side problem, focusing on insufficient household spending, but paid less attention to how supply-side constraints – such as rigid labour markets, low productivity in services and barriers to private sector expansion – shape consumption capacity.
Although an overemphasis on investment and industrial upgrading may have led to low consumption, these strategies were necessary for China’s long-term economic transformation. Moving up value chains requires sustained capital expenditure, and this investment cannot simply be reduced without affecting economic stability.
China’s rapidly ageing population must also be taken into account. Its shrinking workforce and rising dependency ratio all depress consumption through precautionary saving and reduced household confidence. Consumption can’t be boosted with policy levers alone, as no amount of short-term stimulus can fully offset the demographic drag.
If there is to be stronger household income growth, reduced inequality and rebalancing away from state-owned enterprises, the political-economic obstacles to such reforms must be addressed. These include entrenched local government reliance on land finance and the dominant role of state-owned enterprises in strategic sectors.
A fuller analysis of China’s consumption weakness requires confronting the full scale of the complexity of economic rebalancing beyond simply boosting household demand.
Manjit Bhatia, Hanover, New Hampshire, US

data.title: Russia’s Su-57 offer boosts India’s ‘strategic options’ amid strained US ties
data.description:
Russia’s offer to transfer fifth-generation fighter jet technology to India is being seen as a strategic overture at a time when New Delhi’s relations with Washington are strained over trade disputes and diplomatic spats.
Analysts say the proposed transfer – involving the Su-57 stealth fighter – not only signals Moscow’s desire to revitalise defence ties with one of its top arms buyers, but also offers India a timely opportunity to demonstrate that it has “strategic options” as it navigates a balancing act between rival powers.
Moscow’s ambassador to Delhi, Denis Alipov, told Russian state news agency Tass on November 20 that both sides were conducting “intensive work” on the Su-57E platform and that Russia was ready to offer what he described as “unprecedented” localisation.
Sergey Chemezov, chief executive of Rostec – Russia’s state-owned defence and industrial conglomerate – also told Tass that Moscow was prepared to share Su-57 technology without conditions, citing the close partnership between the two countries.
“We are supplying India with all kinds of military equipment according to its needs and strengthening future cooperation,” he said on November 19.
Their remarks come ahead of Russian President Vladimir Putin’s visit to India next month and follow earlier indications of Moscow’s willingness to deepen collaboration. Reuters reported in February that Russia’s state arms exporter had outlined the possibility of licensed Su-57 production in India with full technology transfer.
If accepted, the proposal would mark the first time Delhi has been granted such deep access to fifth-generation fighter technology by any country, according to local media.

Daniel Markey, a senior fellow with the South Asia and China programmes at the Stimson Centre, said for India, this was a welcome gesture from Russia, given Delhi’s recent frustrations with Washington over difficult trade negotiations and US President Donald Trump’s “credit-taking” for the ceasefire with Pakistan.
“Russian weaponry – especially the BrahMos missiles and S-400 air defence systems – served India very well in its May conflict with Pakistan. So India is open to new deals with Russia,” Markey said.
In response to the India-Pakistan conflict which began in April, Trump claimed credit the following month for the ceasefire, saying his administration had mediated talks and used the threat of high trade tariffs to force both countries to de-escalate.
The BrahMos missile system is an Indo-Russian supersonic cruise missile joint venture, while the S-400 is a Russian-developed long-range surface-to-air missile system acquired by India.
The Russian Su-57 jet, considered a counter to the US F-35, is a fifth-generation fighter characterised by its stealth, manoeuvrability and advanced avionics.
The US has long sought to sell the F-35 to India, but Delhi has rejected the offer amid the imposition of US tariffs on India and Washington’s repeated criticism of Delhi’s close ties with Moscow.
For decades, Russia has been India’s primary military supplier, from fighter jets and submarines to missile systems and helicopters.
While both the US and Russia had been “courting” India, Markey said the F-35 offer was “not serious” as no groundwork had been laid to advance that deal.
He added that India wanted to show both Moscow and Washington that it had “strategic options and will not be solely dependent on either”.

Russia, Markey added, was eager to demonstrate its close ties with India even as Delhi appeared to be reducing its purchases of Russian oil under threat of sanctions by the US.
“India has long been a major defence market for Russia and this moment offers Moscow the opportunity to reverse years of a downward slide in its share of that market,” he said.
“Russian defence exports are an important industry and one that Moscow is eager to preserve despite growing competition from other countries.”
The proportion of Russian arms in India’s imports fell from 76 per cent in the 2009-2013 period to 36 per cent in 2019-2023, partly due to India diversifying its arms supply relationships to reduce dependence on a single country.
India is also focusing on its domestic defence industry and increasing self-reliance through the “Make in India” initiative.
Aleksei Zakharov, fellow in the Strategic Studies Programme at the Observer Research Foundation in Delhi, said Russia realised that India was “looking for solutions” in its bid to upgrade its fighter jet fleet.
“Russia recognises that the provision of access to defence technology and the cost of its defence systems are its strengths and is counting on these factors to pitch its fighter jets and other platforms,” Zakharov said.
India is upgrading its fighter jet fleet through a multipronged approach that includes planned acquisitions of foreign advanced fighters, and developing indigenous aircraft such as the AMCA Mk2, an advanced, twin-engine, fifth-generation stealth fighter jet.
It is also upgrading existing aircraft like the Su-30MKI, a twin-engine, twin-seat, all-weather multirole fighter aircraft developed by Russia’s Sukhoi and built under licence by Hindustan Aeronautics for the Indian Air Force.
The upgrading drive is said to be fuelled by the need to replace retiring aircraft and address a squadron shortfall, while also keeping pace with regional adversaries.

Analysts note that with India strengthening its defence ties globally in the past two decades, greater access to American, European and Israeli defence technologies has made Russian suppliers willing to offer “more competitive terms” to procure contracts with India.
“[This] can help [Russia] sustain their defence manufacturing and also their economy,” said Swaran Singh, professor of diplomacy and disarmament at the School of International Studies in Jawaharlal Nehru University.
Moscow’s need to “cultivate” India stemmed from its international isolation since the Ukraine invasion in 2022, he added.
Bilateral trade between India and Russia soared to a record high of about US$68.7 billion in the financial year 2024-25, a nearly six-fold increase from pre-pandemic levels of around US$10 billion.
This rapid increase is driven primarily by a surge in India’s imports of Russian crude oil at discounted prices.
But under pressure from the Americans to buy less oil from Russia, India last week signed a deal to import 2.2 million tons of liquefied petroleum gas annually from the US.
“This has pushed both India and Russia to explore alternative avenues for sustaining a balanced economic partnership,” Singh said.
India’s focus on indigenisation and diversification of its defence procurements also prompted Russia to be “more generous”, including agreeing to Delhi’s terms on joint innovation, research and development and production of advanced defence platforms and technologies, Singh added.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks close in on 2-week high as hopes of US rate cuts fuel rebound
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks approached a two-week high on Thursday as hopes of an interest-rate cut in the US continued to power a rebound from sell-offs triggered by concerns about frenzied sentiment on artificial intelligence.
The Hang Seng Index rose 0.4 per cent to 26,023.23 as of 10.21am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index gained 0.7 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index climbed 0.7 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index added 0.6 per cent.
Plush toymaker Pop Mart International Group rallied 5 per cent to HK$214.80 and chipmaker Semiconductor Manufacturing International gained 3.6 per cent to HK$71.35. Xiaomi added 2.8 per cent to HK$41.24 and electric vehicle maker Li Auto rose 1.9 per cent to HK$73.
Hong Kong stocks have been taking cues from US equities, as optimism about a reduction in the interest rate by the Federal Reserve next month fuels a recovery in risk appetite. Several Fed officials flagged their backing of a cutback in the near term. Expectations about more loosening have been reinforced after a media report surfaced that Kevin Hassett, White House National Economic Council director, was the front-runner for the next Fed Chair, an option that would align with President Donald Trump’s demand for more rate cuts.
Quantgroup Holding, which offers digital empowerment services, jumped 116 per cent to HK$21.18 on its first day of trading in Hong Kong.
Other major Asian markets edged higher. Japan’s Nikkei 225 climbed 1.2 per cent, South Korea’s Kospi rose 1.3 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 added 0.2 per cent.

data.title: Canned soup maker Campbell’s denies 3D-printed chicken claim as it fires US executive
data.description:
Food giant Campbell’s said on Wednesday it has fired an executive who was recorded making racist comments and mocking the company’s products and customers.
Martin Bally, a vice-president in Campbell’s information security department, was named in a lawsuit filed last week by Robert Garza, a former Campbell’s employee who said he was fired January 30 after he reported Bally’s comments to a supervisor.
The lawsuit was filed in Michigan, where both Garza and Bally live. Campbell’s is based in Camden, New Jersey.
In the lawsuit, Garza claimed he met with Bally in November 2024 to discuss his salary. During the meeting, which Garza allegedly recorded, Bally described Campbell’s as “highly process(ed) food” and said it was for “poor people”.
Garza claimed in the lawsuit that Bally made racist remarks about Indian workers, whom he called “idiots”. Garza said Bally also told him that he often went to work high after consuming marijuana edibles.
Campbell’s said Wednesday it first learned of Garza’s lawsuit last week. After listening to portions of the recording, Campbell’s said it believed the voice was Bally’s. Bally was fired on Tuesday.
“The comments were vulgar, offensive and false, and we apologise for the hurt they have caused,” the company said in a statement. “This behaviour does not reflect our values and the culture of our company, and we will not tolerate that kind of language under any circumstances.”
Garza’s lawyer did not respond when Associated Press asked for a copy of the audio recording.
But according to Local 4 news in Detroit, which interviewed Garza and played a portion of the recording on air, Bally said Campbell’s products were unhealthy during his expletive-filled rant.
“Bioengineered meat. I don’t want to eat a piece of chicken that came from a 3D printer,” Bally said.
Campbell’s defended its chicken Wednesday, saying it comes from long-trusted US suppliers, is raised without antibiotics and meets high quality standards.
“The comments heard on the recording about our food are not only inaccurate, they are patently absurd,” Campbell’s said.
Garza is seeking monetary damages from Campbell’s, Bally and from his former manager, J.D. Aupperle. Garza said he told Aupperle about the conversation with Bally shortly before he was fired.
Campbell’s said Wednesday that Aupperle remains employed by the company.

data.title: Hong Kong fancy dress squash event serves up HK$390,000 for Operation Santa Claus
data.description:
Squash players in comical outfits swung their rackets nonstop with enthusiasm at an annual fundraising tournament last week to support vulnerable communities in Hong Kong.
Held at the Hong Kong Football Club (HKFC) squash centre, the 27th Wing Ding Squash Charity Tournament has raised HK$390,000 so far, with a goal of reaching HK$500,000 for Operation Santa Claus (OSC), an annual citywide fundraising initiative run by the South China Morning Post and public broadcaster RTHK since 1988.
Stephen Gollop, chairman of the HKFC squash section’s charity subcommittee and organiser of the event, said 182 players from 14 teams, aged eight to 80, took part this year.
“What we’re trying this year and going forward is to try and make the thing as inclusive as possible,” he said.
Teams wore unique costumes – with a Ronald McDonald, a life-size shark and Santa Claus among those competing across seven courts.
Unlike typical best-of-five games, each about 40-minute match in Wing Ding required players to score as many points as possible, with costumes adding up to 40 extra points.
“It’s not a serious squash competition,” Gollop said.
“People want to win, but quite often the best team doesn’t necessarily win because they have had the hardest fancy dress to play squash in. Winning is great for any team, but it’s the fun element that leads it into a charity event.”
Phil Head, co-founder of the Wing Ding tournament, described it as likely to be the largest charity squash event of its kind in the world.
He started Wing Ding with Nick Rickett in memory of Yuen Kam-wing, a physically impaired and much-loved member of the Hong Kong Squash Centre who died in 1998.
The event drew both local and international stars, including world number 16 Greg Lobban this year, as well as past participants like former world number one Thierry Lincou.
Dressed as Peter Pan and a participant since the tournament’s inception, Head said he aimed to form an international team from Asia to further broaden the event’s inclusivity.
“It’s not just for fun; there’s a worthy cause behind it. It’s about giving back to the squash community and to Hong Kong,” he said.
As founder and owner of Blade Squash International, a squash and racquetball equipment manufacturer, Head’s team competed and also sponsored the lucky draw prizes, adding another layer of support to the fundraising efforts.

Other teams in the Wing Ding tournament included Island Squash Racket Club (ISRC) Helpbnk, Pelican HKFC, St James’s Place (SJP) Spankers, Hong Kong Football Club (HKFC) Lau’s Select, Ladies Recreation Club, Hill Dickinson HKFC, Burke & Co, Kowloon Cricket Club (KCC), Hong Kong Cricket Club (HKCC) Premier Select, “Time for Tea” Macaroon SC, HKCC Smash Squad, Barbarian Beach Brawlers, United Services Recreation Club and HK Squash Gear.
The event also attracted six corporate sponsors: St James’s Place Charitable Foundation, Burke & Company Solicitors, Pelican Financial, HelpBnk, Hill Dickinson Hong Kong and Time for Tea.
Charles Li Tak-kwong, founder and CEO of Pelican Financial, both sponsored and played for a team that raised the second-highest total. He emphasised that effective storytelling and offering benchmark gifts were crucial for encouraging donations.
“I joined the tournament first to play squash and second to support charity,” Li said, dressed in a white office worker costume.
He added: “To me, the most important thing is to give people an opportunity through donations. Especially for those who are poor or in need, they rely on others to provide that chance.”
This year’s OSC theme, “Building Bridges”, aims to foster empathy and connection across Hong Kong. The initiative is supporting 13 local charities that assist children, youth, the elderly and individuals with physical disabilities or mental health challenges.
Learn more about how to donate to OSC at: https://oschk.org/donate

data.title: Malaysia rescues Indonesian helper after years of abuse and unpaid labour
data.description:
An Indonesian domestic worker forced into unpaid labour was rescued by Malaysia’s Immigration Department after a raid at a home in Batu Caves, Selangor state.
In a statement on Wednesday, immigration director general Zakaria Shaaban said the victim was rescued during an operation on Monday.
He said the operation followed information gathered by sources.
“During the inspection, the woman was found hiding under a four-wheel drive vehicle at the premises,” he said.
Zakaria said investigations showed she hid on her employer’s instructions to avoid detection.
“The 56-year-old had worked as a domestic helper for about 16 years without a valid pass. Based on her statement, she was promised 600 ringgit (US$145) a month but had not been paid for the past two years,” Zakaria added.
Zakaria said the victim was traumatised by abuse and barred from contacting her family or returning home.
“She was also forced to work without rest, to the point she had to hide just to sleep. During the operation, a 51-year-old Malaysian man, the employer, was arrested,” he said.
He said the employer admitted obtaining her services through a close family member.
Zakaria added that firm and uncompromising action will be taken against parties involved in human trafficking.
This story was first published by The Star
